title: How to use iterators with for loop

content.problemStatement:
The `for` loop makes iteration easier as it automatically handles the iteration and stops when there are no more elements to iterate over. 

Here's an example of using iterators with a `for` loop. In this example:

- We have a list `my_list` containing integers.
- We create an iterator from the list using the `iter()` function.
- We use a `for` loop to iterate over each element of the list using the iterator.
- Inside the loop, `item` takes each value of the elements in `my_list`, and we print it.

This achieves the same result as accessing elements using `next()` in a loop but in a more Pythonic and concise way. The `for` loop automatically stops when there are no more elements to iterate over.
constraints:
- $1 \leq T \leq 100$
- $1 \leq N \leq 100$
- $1 \leq M \leq N\cdot(N-1)/2$
- $1 \leq u_i, v_i \leq N$
- $u_i \neq v_i$ for each $1 \leq i \leq M$.
- The sum of $N$ over all test cases won't exceed $100$.
content.inputFormat:
- The first line of input will contain a single integer $T$, denoting the number of test cases.
- Each test case consists of multiple lines of input.
    - The first line of each test case contains two space-separated integers $N$ and $M$ — the number of vertices and edges of the graph, respectively.
    - The next $M$ lines describe the edges. The $i$-th of these $M$ lines contains two space-separated integers $u_i$ and $v_i$, denoting an edge between $u_i$ and $v_i$.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output on a new line the number of good subgraphs of $G$, modulo $10^9 + 7$.
